posts.text:
It’s hard to overstate how rare it is to see Dem turnout in a midterm primary election exceeding that of a presidential election.


Tom Bonier (@tbonier)

Wow, the Dem primary turnout among Hispanic voters in Texas is going to be giving the GOP sweats for the next 8 months. 

Take Zapata County - turnout in the Dem primary there yesterday was 143% the total number of votes Harris won in the 2024 general election. That's wild.



— https://nitter.net/tbonier/status/2029162284942024705#m

posts.text: Not every vote is in (the outlined counties don't have >95% counted, and Maverick hasn't reported at all), but this shows the swing between Harris' margin in '24 and the Dem margin in yesterday's primary turnout. You can see in south Texas the shifts were >100%.
posts.quote: Not every vote is in (the outlined counties don't have >95% counted, and Maverick hasn't reported at all), but this shows the swing between Harris' margin in '24 and the Dem margin in yesterday's primary turnout. You can see in south Texas the shifts were >100%.

posts.text: Let's keep in mind, the GOP reportedly spent upwards of $80 million on their primaries in Texas, well more than double the amount spent by Dems.
posts.quote: Let's keep in mind, the GOP reportedly spent upwards of $80 million on their primaries in Texas, well more than double the amount spent by Dems.

posts.text: Here are 4 districts the TX GOP gerrymandered to gain seats in November, with the Dem and GOP primary vote. Dems outvoted GOPs in every one of them.

posts.text: Republicans seem to in denial of the wild unpopularity of their actions, and the looming political disaster they are speedwalking into.
posts.quote: Republicans seem to in denial of the wild unpopularity of their actions, and the looming political disaster they are speedwalking into.

posts.text: In 2020 GOP turnout exceeded Dem by 7.1%, and by 6.4% in 2022 and 2024. Yet in 2025, Dem turnout in PA exceed GOP turnout by 8%. That's a swing of close to 15 pts in favor of Dems, just in turnout.
posts.quote: In 2020 GOP turnout exceeded Dem by 7.1%, and by 6.4% in 2022 and 2024. Yet in 2025, Dem turnout in PA exceed GOP turnout by 8%. That's a swing of close to 15 pts in favor of Dems, just in turnout.

posts.text:
We have the turnout data now from the 2025 election in PA that saw the Dems sweep the statewide judicial races, as well as downballot races. 

Dem turnout swamped a depressed GOP base. If I were GOPs, I'd be more than a little worried about the upcoming midterms.
posts.quote:
We have the turnout data now from the 2025 election in PA that saw the Dems sweep the statewide judicial races, as well as downballot races. 

Dem turnout swamped a depressed GOP base. If I were GOPs, I'd be more than a little worried about the upcoming midterms.

posts.text: As far as total turnout by party,  here are the differentials from 2021 to 2025. GOP turnout actually increased over 2021, but only slightly. Dem turnout saw a massive increase of 14.7 pts.
posts.quote: As far as total turnout by party,  here are the differentials from 2021 to 2025. GOP turnout actually increased over 2021, but only slightly. Dem turnout saw a massive increase of 14.7 pts.

posts.text: There was a massive youth vote surge in NJ this year. In 2021 voters under 30 accounted for 7.1% of all votes cast. This year they were 11.5% of the electorate.
posts.quote: There was a massive youth vote surge in NJ this year. In 2021 voters under 30 accounted for 7.1% of all votes cast. This year they were 11.5% of the electorate.

posts.text: Look at turnout by age/party in New Jersey - Dems outperformed GOPs in turnout % among every age <45, with the biggest advantage among 21 and 22 year olds (+10% Dem turnout).
posts.quote: Look at turnout by age/party in New Jersey - Dems outperformed GOPs in turnout % among every age <45, with the biggest advantage among 21 and 22 year olds (+10% Dem turnout).

posts.text: Past special elections with massive swings to Dems could be explained away by GOPs, because turnout was relatively low. Not this one. Republicans should be deeply concerned.
posts.quote: Past special elections with massive swings to Dems could be explained away by GOPs, because turnout was relatively low. Not this one. Republicans should be deeply concerned.

posts.text: There you have it. With Davidson County reporting every precinct now, TN7 turnout is at 179,634, just barely short of the 181,822 turnout in the 2022 general election. I wouldn't be surprised if it exceeds it when all votes are counted.
posts.quote: There you have it. With Davidson County reporting every precinct now, TN7 turnout is at 179,634, just barely short of the 181,822 turnout in the 2022 general election. I wouldn't be surprised if it exceeds it when all votes are counted.

What's more, the fact that Dem share of the primary turnout in Hispanic counties was so massive is clearly a strong indicator that Hispanics swung back to Dems. If you can't see how that might impact the general election, I cannot help you.

Sometimes it's not that complicated. Like in the case of Zapata County, TX where 2,700 Dem votes were cast in the primary. That's more votes than Harris, or Beto x2, or Biden won in their November general elections. Historically, almost every primary voter will vote in November.

It’s hard to overstate how rare it is to see Dem turnout in a midterm primary election exceeding that of a presidential election.


Tom Bonier (@tbonier)

Wow, the Dem primary turnout among Hispanic voters in Texas is going to be giving the GOP sweats for the next 8 months. 

Take Zapata County - tu

Not every vote is in (the outlined counties don't have >95% counted, and Maverick hasn't reported at all), but this shows the swing between Harris' margin in '24 and the Dem margin in yesterday's primary turnout. You can see in south Texas the shifts were >100%.
Let's keep in mind, the GOP reportedly spent upwards of $80 million on their primaries in Texas, well more than double the amount spent by Dems.
Here are 4 districts the TX GOP gerrymandered to gain seats in November, with the Dem and GOP primary vote. Dems outvoted GOPs in every one of them.

Wow, the Dem primary turnout among Hispanic voters in Texas is going to be giving the GOP sweats for the next 8 months. 

Take Zapata County - turnout in the Dem primary there yesterday was 143% the total number of votes Harris won in the 2024 general election. That's wild.

It's still (slightly) early in TX, but it looks like the public polls I referenced in this thread were decent, and yes, the early vote in TX was generally favorable for Talarico, thanks to a younger than average vote, and his strength with white and Hispanic voters.

If I'm a Republican in TX, this part would make me especially nervous, given that they are counting on maintaining 2024 Trump levels of support among Latino voters in order to pick up two gerrymandered congressional seats.

Some notes:
- Black women are leading the way with the biggest Dem advantage across every age group
- Black men are also showing huge Dem margins
- Dems lead in every single group of under age 30 voters, even white men
- GOPs don't have an advantage with any group <50

I made this monster of a chart to show the partisan split of the early/abs vote in the TX primary by age/race/gender. 

You can see why Republicans must be worried about what they're seeing in the turnout data.

Not every vote is in (the outlined counties don't have >95% counted, and Maverick hasn't reported at all), but this shows the swing between Harris' margin in '24 and the Dem margin in yesterday's primary turnout. You can see in south Texas the shifts were >100%.
Let's keep in mind, the GOP reportedly spent upwards of $80 million on their primaries in Texas, well more than double the amount spent by Dems.
Here are 4 districts the TX GOP gerrymandered to gain seats in November, with the Dem and GOP primary vote. Dems outvoted GOPs in every one of them.

Wow, the Dem primary turnout among Hispanic voters in Texas is going to be giving the GOP sweats for the next 8 months. 

Take Zapata County - turnout in the Dem primary there yesterday was 143% the total number of votes Harris won in the 2024 general election. That's wild.
Here's a better version with it sorted by partisanship gaps, and with the N size included for each group

Some notes:
- Black women are leading the way with the biggest Dem advantage across every age group
- Black men are also showing huge Dem margins
- Dems lead in every single group of under age 30 voters, even white men
- GOPs don't have an advantage with any group <50

I made this monster of a chart to show the partisan split of the early/abs vote in the TX primary by age/race/gender. 

You can see why Republicans must be worried about what they're seeing in the turnout data.
In 2020 GOP turnout exceeded Dem by 7.1%, and by 6.4% in 2022 and 2024. Yet in 2025, Dem turnout in PA exceed GOP turnout by 8%. That's a swing of close to 15 pts in favor of Dems, just in turnout.

We have the turnout data now from the 2025 election in PA that saw the Dems sweep the statewide judicial races, as well as downballot races. 

Dem turnout swamped a depressed GOP base. If I were GOPs, I'd be more than a little worried about the upcoming midterms.
As far as total turnout by party,  here are the differentials from 2021 to 2025. GOP turnout actually increased over 2021, but only slightly. Dem turnout saw a massive increase of 14.7 pts.
There was a massive youth vote surge in NJ this year. In 2021 voters under 30 accounted for 7.1% of all votes cast. This year they were 11.5% of the electorate.
Look at turnout by age/party in New Jersey - Dems outperformed GOPs in turnout % among every age <45, with the biggest advantage among 21 and 22 year olds (+10% Dem turnout).
Past special elections with massive swings to Dems could be explained away by GOPs, because turnout was relatively low. Not this one. Republicans should be deeply concerned.

There you have it. With Davidson County reporting every precinct now, TN7 turnout is at 179,634, just barely short of the 181,822 turnout in the 2022 general election. I wouldn't be surprised if it exceeds it when all votes are counted.
